body_txt: It's the final quarter. The game is still too close to call. The clock is counting down. There are seconds to go. You have the ball. It's all up to you. And you stand there. Think back to your training. Analyze the best response. And do absolutely nothing in the meantime. Friend, you just choked. Any time you're doing something you're accustomed to, something you're good at. Hell, you can be a recognized master in your field, but as soon as the pressure is on, you can choke. It can happen in sports. It can happen on game shows. It can happen in bed. Expert athletes often talk about getting into "the zone", when they stop thinking about the actions of what they're doing, and just do it . This usually leads to perfect performances that crush the other team easily. Conversely, choking occurs when, instead of getting into the zone, the expert starts thinking about what they're doing; thinking about thinking essentially. They start trying to control everything , trying to account for every last variable. They get inside their own head, and because there's just too much to think about, they can't do anything. They've got the yips or, as The Other Wiki calls it, focal dystonia . Choice Paralysis describes a similar situation where a person is so overwhelmed by how many options they're presented with, that they cannot choose what or who to go for — paralysed, if you will. Compare with the Centipede's Dilemma . Contrast Awesomeness by Analysis .
